facts.text: Ludwig van Beethoven continued composing masterpieces even after becoming almost completely deaf by age 44.
A standard symphony orchestra typically has between 70 and 100 musicians.
Antonio Vivaldi was ordained as a priest and was known as 'The Red Priest' because of his red hair.
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart was born in Salzburg, Austria.
The piano has 88 keys — 52 white and 36 black — spanning over seven octaves.
Johann Sebastian Bach fathered 20 children, several of whom became notable composers themselves.
Carnegie Hall in New York City opened in 1891 with Tchaikovsky conducting the inaugural concert.
The word 'symphony' comes from the Greek 'symphonia,' meaning 'sounding together.'
Niccolò Paganini was such a virtuoso violinist that some believed he had made a deal with the devil.
The Vienna Musikverein's Golden Hall is considered one of the finest concert halls in the world for its acoustics.
Frédéric Chopin composed almost exclusively for solo piano, producing over 200 works for the instrument.
The Baroque period in music lasted roughly from 1600 to 1750.
Beethoven's Symphony No. 9 premiered in Vienna.
A Stradivarius violin made by Antonio Stradivari in Cremona, Italy, can sell for over $15 million at auction.
The oboe is typically the instrument used to tune the orchestra before a performance.
Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky's 'The Nutcracker' ballet premiered in St. Petersburg in 1892.
The conductor's baton evolved from a rolled-up piece of paper used to keep time in the 17th century.
George Frideric Handel composed 'Messiah' in just 24 days.
The celesta, a keyboard instrument with a bell-like tone, was used by Tchaikovsky in the 'Dance of the Sugar Plum Fairy.'
Mozart wrote his first symphony at the age of eight.
The concertmaster is the first-chair violinist and serves as the leader of the orchestra after the conductor.
Sergei Rachmaninoff had exceptionally large hands, reportedly able to span 12 piano keys with one hand.
Ludwig van Beethoven was born in Bonn, Germany.
The French horn has about 12 to 13 feet of tubing coiled into its circular shape.
Richard Wagner's 'Ring Cycle' is a set of four operas that takes about 15 hours to perform in full.
Yo-Yo Ma began studying cello at age four and performed for Presidents Kennedy and Eisenhower at age seven.
The term 'fortissimo' in music means to play very loudly.
Gustav Holst's orchestral suite 'The Planets' does not include Earth among its seven movements.
The Romantic period in classical music spanned roughly from 1820 to 1900.
Antonio Stradivari crafted approximately 1,116 instruments in his lifetime, of which about 650 survive today.
Johann Sebastian Bach was born in Eisenach, Germany.
The double bass is the largest and lowest-pitched string instrument in a standard orchestra.
Claude Debussy is often considered the founder of musical Impressionism.
Luciano Pavarotti was known as 'King of the High Cs' for his powerful and reliable top notes.
A fugue is a compositional technique where a theme is introduced and then developed through multiple voices.
The Berlin Philharmonie, opened in 1963, was revolutionary for its vineyard-style seating surrounding the stage.
Franz Schubert composed over 600 songs (Lieder) during his short life of just 31 years.
The piccolo is the highest-pitched instrument in a standard orchestra.
Handel's 'Messiah' contains the famous 'Hallelujah' chorus, during which audiences traditionally stand.
Igor Stravinsky's 'The Rite of Spring' caused a riot at its Paris premiere.
The Boehm system, developed in the 1840s, revolutionized the flute's key mechanism and remains the standard today.
Herbert von Karajan conducted the Berlin Philharmonic for 35 years, from 1954 to 1989.
A sonata typically consists of three or four movements with contrasting tempos and moods.
Johannes Brahms spent 21 years composing his First Symphony, partly out of anxiety about following in Beethoven's footsteps.
Maria Callas is widely regarded as one of the most influential opera singers of the 20th century.
The Classical period in music lasted roughly from 1750 to 1820.
Steinway & Sons, founded in 1853, builds each piano from approximately 12,000 individual parts.
Antonín Dvořák's 'New World Symphony' was composed while he lived in the United States, drawing on American folk melodies.
The contrabassoon plays an octave lower than the standard bassoon and is the lowest-pitched woodwind.
Frédéric Chopin was born near Warsaw, Poland.
Leonard Bernstein was the first American-born conductor to lead a major U.S. symphony orchestra.
The timpani, or kettledrums, are the most commonly used percussion instruments in an orchestra.
Giuseppe Verdi's opera 'La Traviata' was initially a failure at its 1853 premiere in Venice.
Bach's 'Toccata and Fugue in D minor' is one of the most recognized organ works ever composed.
The English horn is neither English nor a horn — it is actually an alto oboe.
Jascha Heifetz is often considered the greatest violinist of the 20th century.
Rondo form features a recurring main theme that alternates with contrasting sections, often labeled ABACA.
The Sydney Opera House, designed by Jørn Utzon, opened in 1973 and hosts over 1,500 performances yearly.
Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky was born in Votkinsk, Russia.
Felix Mendelssohn helped revive interest in Bach's music by conducting the St. Matthew Passion in 1829, nearly a century after Bach's death.
A standard orchestra is divided into four sections: strings, woodwinds, brass, and percussion.
Giacomo Puccini's 'La Bohème' tells the story of struggling artists in 1830s Paris.
Vladimir Horowitz was considered one of the greatest pianists of the 20th century, famous for his electric performances.
The harp has 47 strings and seven foot pedals that allow it to play in any key.
Beethoven's 'Moonlight Sonata' was originally titled 'Piano Sonata No. 14 in C-sharp minor.'
The Medieval period of Western music spans roughly from 500 to 1400 AD.
Maurice Ravel's 'Boléro' builds from a single quiet snare drum rhythm to a massive orchestral crescendo over 15 minutes.
The Guarneri family of Cremona produced violins that rival Stradivarius instruments in quality and value.
Gustavo Dudamel became music director of the Los Angeles Philharmonic at just 28 years old.
Pachelbel's Canon in D has become one of the most popular pieces for weddings, though it was largely forgotten for centuries.
The trombone is unique among brass instruments because it uses a slide rather than valves to change pitch.
Franz Joseph Haydn is often called the 'Father of the Symphony' for establishing the form's standard structure.
Dvořák's 'New World Symphony' premiered at Carnegie Hall.
Debussy's 'Clair de Lune' is the third movement of his 'Suite bergamasque.'
Itzhak Perlman has performed at four U.S. presidential inaugurations.
The word 'piano' is short for 'pianoforte,' meaning 'soft-loud' in Italian.
Robert Schumann injured his right hand using a mechanical finger-strengthening device, ending his career as a pianist.
A key signature at the beginning of a musical staff tells performers which notes to play sharp or flat throughout.
The Royal Albert Hall in London can seat over 5,200 people and hosts the BBC Proms each summer.
Dmitri Shostakovich composed 15 symphonies and 15 string quartets during the Soviet era.
The Zildjian cymbal company was founded in Constantinople in 1623, making it over 400 years old.
George Frideric Handel was born in Halle, Germany.
The viola is slightly larger than the violin and plays in a lower register, providing the orchestra's inner harmonic voice.
Arturo Toscanini was famous for conducting entirely from memory due to his severe nearsightedness.
Mozart composed over 600 works in his short 35-year life, including 41 symphonies and 27 piano concertos.
The brass section of a full orchestra typically includes French horns, trumpets, trombones, and a tuba.
Beethoven originally dedicated his Third Symphony ('Eroica') to Napoleon but scratched out the dedication when Napoleon declared himself Emperor.
Georges Bizet's opera 'Carmen' was initially considered scandalous for its portrayal of a seductive, independent woman.
The Renaissance period in music lasted approximately from 1400 to 1600.
Hilary Hahn began studying violin at age three and made her orchestral debut at eleven with the Baltimore Symphony.
The Concertgebouw in Amsterdam, opened in 1888, is renowned for its near-perfect acoustics.
Franz Liszt is considered the first true rock star of classical music, with fans fainting at his concerts in a phenomenon called 'Lisztomania.'
Vivaldi composed over 500 concertos, including 'The Four Seasons,' a set of four violin concertos.
The bassoon's double reed and long wooden body give it a distinctive warm, dark tone.
Tchaikovsky's '1812 Overture' calls for actual cannon fire in its score.
Johann Sebastian Bach died in Leipzig, Germany.
Plácido Domingo has performed more than 150 different operatic roles over his career, a record number.
In a concerto, a solo instrument is featured alongside the full orchestra.
Bösendorfer pianos, made in Vienna since 1828, are known for their rich, warm bass tones.
Sergei Prokofiev's 'Peter and the Wolf' assigns different instruments to each character in the story.
The clarinet has the widest range of any common woodwind instrument, spanning nearly four octaves.
Stanley Kubrick's '2001: A Space Odyssey' famously uses Richard Strauss's 'Also sprach Zarathustra' and Johann Strauss II's 'The Blue Danube.'
Gustav Mahler's symphonies are among the longest in the standard repertoire, with his Third Symphony lasting over 90 minutes.
The trumpet has been used in music for over 3,500 years, with ancient examples found in Egypt.
Felix Mendelssohn was born in Hamburg, Germany.
Arthur Rubinstein was celebrated for his interpretations of Chopin and performed publicly for over 75 years.
The string section is the largest section of the orchestra, usually comprising more than half the musicians.
Mozart's opera 'The Magic Flute' combines Masonic symbolism with fairy-tale elements.
Sir Georg Solti holds the record for the most Grammy Awards won by any individual, with 31 wins.
The organ is often called the 'King of Instruments' because of its enormous range and power.
Jacqueline du Pré's recording of Elgar's Cello Concerto is considered one of the greatest classical recordings ever made.
Dynamics in music range from 'pianissimo' (very soft) to 'fortissimo' (very loud).
Beethoven's Fifth Symphony opens with one of the most famous four-note motifs in all of music.
The cello's full name is 'violoncello,' meaning 'small large viol' in Italian.
Disney's 'Fantasia' (1940) paired animated sequences with classical works by Bach, Beethoven, Stravinsky, and others.
Richard Wagner built his own opera house, the Bayreuth Festspielhaus, specifically to stage his works.
Franz Schubert was born in Vienna, Austria.
The harpsichord was the dominant keyboard instrument before the piano was invented around 1700.
Zubin Mehta became music director of the Los Angeles Philharmonic at age 26, the youngest person to hold such a post at the time.
Rossini's 'The Barber of Seville' is one of the most frequently performed comic operas in the world.
A symphony typically has four movements: a fast opening, a slow second, a dance-like third, and a fast finale.
Lang Lang has been credited with inspiring over 100 million Chinese children to learn the piano.
The tuba is the largest and lowest-pitched brass instrument in the orchestra.
Chopin requested that Mozart's Requiem be performed at his funeral, and it was.
The woodwind section typically includes flutes, oboes, clarinets, and bassoons.
Beethoven's only opera, 'Fidelio,' went through multiple revisions and three different overtures.
Fazioli pianos, handcrafted in Sacile, Italy, are among the most expensive production pianos in the world.
Vivaldi was born in Venice, Italy.
The 20th-century modern period brought atonality, serialism, and experimental techniques to classical music.
Stravinsky's 'The Firebird' established his international reputation when it premiered in Paris in 1910.
The French horn is considered one of the most difficult orchestral instruments to play due to its complex overtone series.
Puccini's 'Madame Butterfly' is set in Nagasaki, Japan, and tells the tragic story of a geisha named Cio-Cio-San.
The conductor uses specific gestures to indicate tempo, dynamics, cues, and phrasing to the orchestra.
Brahms was born in Hamburg, Germany.
Rachmaninoff's Piano Concerto No. 2 was dedicated to his therapist, who helped him overcome a creative crisis.
Figure skaters frequently perform to classical music, with Bolero, Swan Lake, and Carmen being popular choices.
The bow used for string instruments is traditionally strung with horsehair, usually from the tail of a white horse.
Verdi's 'Aida' was commissioned to celebrate the opening of the Suez Canal, though it premiered after the canal opened.
Debussy was born in Saint-Germain-en-Laye, France.
The percussion section can include timpani, snare drums, bass drums, cymbals, xylophones, glockenspiels, and many more instruments.
Mozart's Requiem was left unfinished at his death and was completed by his student Franz Xaver Süssmayr.
A canon is a compositional technique where a melody is imitated by one or more voices at a set interval.
The Berlin Philharmonic is consistently ranked among the top orchestras in the world.
Shostakovich's Symphony No. 7 ('Leningrad') was composed during the Siege of Leningrad in World War II.
The flute is the oldest known musical instrument, with bone flutes dating back over 40,000 years.
Mahler was born in Kaliště, Bohemia (now the Czech Republic).
Luthiers are craftspeople who build and repair stringed instruments such as violins, violas, and cellos.
Rossini retired from opera at age 37 and spent the remaining 39 years of his life composing very little.
The orchestra's seating arrangement typically places first violins to the conductor's left and cellos to the right.
Beethoven's hearing loss began around age 28 and was nearly total by the time he composed his Ninth Symphony.
The film 'Amadeus' (1984) dramatized a fictional rivalry between Mozart and Antonio Salieri.
Ravel was born in the Basque town of Ciboure, France.
A Steinway grand piano's soundboard is made from Sitka spruce, selected for its resonance properties.
The term 'opus' (plural 'opera') is used to number a composer's works in order of publication.
Prokofiev returned to the Soviet Union in 1936 after years abroad, a decision that subjected him to artistic censorship.
Stradivari's workshop in Cremona, Italy, produced instruments between approximately 1666 and 1737.
Wagner's concept of 'Gesamtkunstwerk' (total work of art) aimed to unify music, drama, and visual arts in opera.
The cello is held between the player's knees and rests on an endpin that touches the floor.
Handel became a British citizen in 1727 and composed the coronation anthem 'Zadok the Priest,' used at every British coronation since.
Tchaikovsky died in St. Petersburg, Russia.
The clarinet was invented around 1700 by Johann Christoph Denner in Nuremberg, Germany.
Schubert's Symphony No. 8, known as the 'Unfinished Symphony,' has only two completed movements.
Video game soundtracks, such as those for 'Final Fantasy' and 'The Legend of Zelda,' draw heavily on classical music techniques.
The Renaissance saw the rise of polyphony, where multiple independent vocal lines were woven together.
Liszt invented the modern solo piano recital format, performing entire concerts alone.
The Vienna Philharmonic did not admit women as full members until 1997.
Dvořák was born in Nelahozeves, Bohemia (now the Czech Republic).
Buffet Crampon, founded in Paris in 1825, is one of the most respected clarinet manufacturers in the world.
Mendelssohn's 'Wedding March' from 'A Midsummer Night's Dream' is one of the most commonly played pieces at weddings.
The pitch standard of A=440 Hz was internationally adopted in 1955, though it had been common since the 1930s.
Brahms was a close friend of Clara Schumann, the pianist wife of Robert Schumann, and their relationship has been widely debated by historians.
The Baroque period introduced opera, the concerto, and the sonata as major musical forms.
Rachmaninoff's hands could reportedly span a 13th interval on the piano, from C to the A an octave above.
Wagner was born in Leipzig, Germany.
The orchestra's woodwind section typically has pairs: two flutes, two oboes, two clarinets, and two bassoons.
Haydn wrote 104 numbered symphonies over his career, earning him the 'Father of the Symphony' title.
Shostakovich's Symphony No. 5 was subtitled 'A Soviet Artist's Creative Response to Just Criticism' to appease the Soviet government.
The 'Dies Irae' chant from the Requiem Mass has been quoted in hundreds of classical and film compositions.
An operatic soprano can sing high notes that shatter glass under the right conditions — the phenomenon involves matching the glass's resonant frequency.
Verdi was born near Busseto, Italy.
The pipe organ at the Boardwalk Hall in Atlantic City has over 33,000 pipes, making it the world's largest.
Prokofiev died on the same day as Joseph Stalin, and his death received little public notice.
Debussy's 'Prélude à l'après-midi d'un faune' is considered a turning point in the history of modern music.
The upright piano was developed in the early 1800s as a space-saving alternative to the grand piano.
A concerto grosso, popular in the Baroque era, features a small group of soloists contrasted with the full orchestra.
Pavarotti's 'Nessun Dorma' performance at the 1990 FIFA World Cup made him an international superstar.
The typical orchestral trumpet is pitched in B-flat, though C trumpets are also common in orchestral settings.
Chopin's 'Minute Waltz' is not actually meant to be played in one minute — its name comes from the word 'minute' meaning 'small.'
Stravinsky was born in Oranienbaum, Russia.
The conductor's podium raises them above the orchestra so every musician can see their gestures clearly.
Bach's 'Well-Tempered Clavier' consists of 48 preludes and fugues, two in each major and minor key.
The modern symphony orchestra reached its largest size during the late Romantic period.
Maria Callas lost over 80 pounds in the early 1950s, dramatically transforming her stage presence and career.
The viola uses an alto clef, making it the only common orchestral string instrument that does not primarily use treble or bass clef.
Schubert died at age 31, possibly from typhoid fever, leaving a treasure trove of unfinished and unpublished works.
Puccini was born in Lucca, Italy.
The metronome was patented by Johann Maelzel in 1815, giving composers a precise way to indicate tempo.
Holst composed 'The Planets' between 1914 and 1917, with each movement representing a planet's astrological character.
The oldest surviving complete musical composition is the 'Seikilos Epitaph,' a Greek melody from around the 1st century AD.
Bernstein's 'West Side Story' brilliantly blended classical composition techniques with Broadway musical theater.
The string quartet — two violins, viola, and cello — is one of the most important chamber music ensembles.
Schumann was born in Zwickau, Saxony (now Germany).
The modern concert grand piano can produce sounds ranging from a whisper to the volume of a full orchestra.
Verdi's 'Requiem' was composed in memory of the Italian writer Alessandro Manzoni.
Rachmaninoff was born in Semyonovo, Russia.
The celesta was invented in 1886 by Auguste Mustel in Paris and produces a delicate, bell-like tone.
A fermata (pause symbol) tells a performer to hold a note longer than its written value, at the performer's discretion.
Wagner's operas pioneered the use of 'leitmotifs' — short musical themes associated with specific characters or ideas.
The Baroque era saw the development of tonal harmony and the major-minor key system still used today.
Yo-Yo Ma plays a 1733 Montagnana cello, one of the finest instruments of its kind.
Mozart was buried in a common grave in Vienna, and the exact location of his remains is unknown.
The snare drum's distinctive buzzing sound comes from metal wires stretched across its bottom head.
Chopin died in Paris, France.
Debussy's whole-tone scale usage created a dreamy, floating quality that broke from traditional tonal harmony.
The orchestra pit in opera houses is positioned below and in front of the stage to keep musicians out of the audience's sightline.
Liszt was born in Raiding, Hungary (now Austria).
Haydn's 'Farewell Symphony' ends with musicians leaving the stage one by one, a hint to his patron that the orchestra wanted to go home.
The tuba was invented in the 1830s in Prussia and gradually replaced the ophicleide in the orchestra.
Ravel's 'Piano Concerto for the Left Hand' was commissioned by pianist Paul Wittgenstein, who lost his right arm in World War I.
Mahler's Symphony No. 8, known as the 'Symphony of a Thousand,' requires over 800 performers.
The bassoon is sometimes called the 'clown of the orchestra' because of its ability to produce comical sounds.
Mendelssohn's overture to 'A Midsummer Night's Dream' was composed when he was just 17 years old.
Mozart died in Vienna, Austria.
Bow makers, called 'archetiers,' shape bows from pernambuco wood, a dense Brazilian hardwood prized for its springiness.
The Classical period emphasized clarity, balance, and form, producing the sonata, symphony, and string quartet.
Shostakovich wrote his String Quartet No. 8 in just three days, reportedly in tears throughout the process.
Vivaldi composed over 230 concertos for violin, making it his most featured instrument.
The xylophone's wooden bars are struck with mallets to produce a bright, sharp tone.
Schubert composed 'Erlkönig,' one of his most famous songs, at the age of 18.
Handel's 'Water Music' was composed for King George I's barge party on the River Thames in 1717.
Shostakovich was born in St. Petersburg, Russia.
The pedal on a piano that sustains notes is called the 'damper pedal' or 'sustain pedal.'
Beethoven's 'Für Elise' was not published until 40 years after his death.
Prokofiev's 'Romeo and Juliet' ballet contains the menacing 'Dance of the Knights,' widely used in film and television.
The Renaissance motet and madrigal were important vocal forms that preceded the development of opera.
Clara Schumann was one of the most distinguished pianists of the Romantic era and a respected composer in her own right.
The glockenspiel uses metal bars instead of wooden ones, producing a bright, tinkling sound.
Tchaikovsky's 'Swan Lake' was a failure at its premiere in 1877 but became the most popular ballet in the world after his death.
Haydn was born in Rohrau, Austria.
The modern orchestral seating arrangement was largely standardized by conductor Leopold Stokowski.
Brahms composed his famous 'Lullaby' (Wiegenlied) in 1868, and it remains one of the most recognized melodies worldwide.
The harpsichord plucks strings rather than striking them, giving it a distinctive, crisp sound.
Puccini's 'Turandot' was left unfinished at his death and was completed by Franco Alfano.
Bach composed the six Brandenburg Concertos as a gift to Margrave Christian Ludwig of Brandenburg.
The principal oboist sets the tuning note (usually an A at 440 Hz) for the rest of the orchestra.
Verdi's name became an acronym for the Italian unification movement: 'Vittorio Emanuele Re D'Italia.'
Liszt's 'Hungarian Rhapsodies' drew on Roma musical traditions and remain staples of the virtuoso piano repertoire.
Beethoven's hearing aid collection included large ear trumpets and a special resonance plate attached to his piano.
Schubert was born in Himmelpfortgrund, now part of Vienna.
The triangle, one of the simplest percussion instruments, can cut through the sound of a full orchestra.
Mahler served as director of the Vienna Court Opera from 1897 to 1907.
The treble clef is also called the G clef because it curls around the second line, which represents the note G.
Dvořák served as director of the National Conservatory of Music in New York City from 1892 to 1895.
The piano was invented around 1700 by Bartolomeo Cristofori in Florence, Italy.
Stravinsky became a U.S. citizen in 1945 and lived in Hollywood for many years.
A crescendo gradually increases volume, while a decrescendo (or diminuendo) gradually decreases it.
The 'New World Symphony' by Dvořák premiered at Carnegie Hall in 1893 and was an immediate sensation.
Prokofiev was born in Sontsivka, Ukraine.
Wagner's opera 'Tristan und Isolde' is considered a pivotal work that pushed tonal harmony to its limits.
The orchestra's brass section can produce the loudest sounds, with the trumpet capable of reaching 110 decibels.
Vivaldi taught music at the Ospedale della Pietà, an orphanage for girls in Venice, for much of his career.
The bass clef is also called the F clef because its two dots surround the fourth line, representing F.
Mendelssohn revived interest in the music of J.S. Bach, who had been largely forgotten after his death.
Beethoven's Ninth Symphony was the first major symphony to include vocal soloists and a chorus.
The cor anglais (English horn) is pitched a fifth lower than the oboe and has a more mellow, haunting tone.
Liszt died in Bayreuth, Germany.
Conductor Carlos Kleiber is often cited by fellow conductors as the greatest of all time, despite recording very few works.
The clavichord, a predecessor of the piano, could produce dynamic variation but was very quiet.
Rachmaninoff's 'Rhapsody on a Theme of Paganini' contains 24 variations on Paganini's famous Caprice No. 24.
The marimba is a larger cousin of the xylophone with resonator tubes beneath each wooden bar.
Schumann's wife Clara premiered many of Brahms's piano works and championed his music throughout her life.
Mozart's opera 'Don Giovanni' was described by its composer as a 'dramma giocoso' — a blend of comedy and drama.
The standard tuning of a violin's four strings, from lowest to highest, is G, D, A, E.
Mendelssohn died in Leipzig, Germany.
Debussy refused to be called an Impressionist, preferring to think of himself as a Symbolist.
The natural horn, without valves, required players to insert their hand into the bell to change pitches.
Bach's 'Goldberg Variations' were allegedly composed to help Count Kaiserling fall asleep.
A cadenza is a virtuosic solo passage near the end of a concerto movement, often improvised in earlier periods.
Puccini's opera 'Tosca' features the dramatic aria 'Vissi d'arte,' a plea from the titular character.
The term 'chamber music' originally referred to music performed in a room or private setting rather than a large concert hall.
Verdi died in Milan, Italy.
The modern grand piano has a cast-iron frame that can withstand over 20 tons of string tension.
Sibelius's 'Finlandia' became a symbol of Finnish national identity during the country's struggle for independence from Russia.
The recorder, not the flute, was the standard orchestral woodwind instrument until the Baroque period.
Dvořák reportedly used themes from African-American spirituals and Native American music in his 'New World Symphony.'
Tchaikovsky's Sixth Symphony ('Pathétique') premiered just nine days before his death.
Each string in a grand piano is under approximately 160 to 200 pounds of tension.
Handel was blind for the last seven years of his life but continued to perform and supervise performances.
Schubert died in Vienna, Austria.
The piccolo plays one octave higher than the flute and adds brilliance to orchestral passages.
Wagner's 'Ride of the Valkyries' is one of the most recognizable pieces of classical music, widely used in films.
A music score's tempo markings use Italian terms: 'adagio' (slow), 'andante' (walking pace), 'allegro' (fast), 'presto' (very fast).
Rachmaninoff fled Russia permanently after the 1917 Revolution and eventually settled in the United States.
The contrabass clarinet plays two octaves below the standard B-flat clarinet.
Mahler converted from Judaism to Catholicism partly to become eligible for the directorship of the Vienna Court Opera.
Chopin never performed a full public concert after the age of 29, preferring intimate salon performances.
Richard Strauss (not to be confused with the waltz Strausses) composed 'Der Rosenkavalier' and 'Also sprach Zarathustra.'
Handel died in London, England.
The sousaphone, a type of tuba designed for marching bands, was created for John Philip Sousa's band.
Mozart's 'Eine kleine Nachtmusik' is one of the most frequently performed pieces in the classical repertoire.
Stravinsky composed music in wildly different styles throughout his career, from Russian folk to neoclassical to serial techniques.
The theremin, an electronic instrument played without physical contact, was used by some 20th-century classical composers.
Brahms's German Requiem is sung in German rather than the traditional Latin, reflecting his Protestant background.
The concept of absolute pitch (perfect pitch) is found in approximately 1 in 10,000 people.
Beethoven died in Vienna, Austria.
The bass drum provides the deepest sound in the percussion section and can be felt as much as heard.
The Metropolitan Opera in New York City is one of the world's premier opera houses, seating nearly 4,000 people.
Schubert's 'Trout Quintet' is named for its fourth movement, which is a set of variations on his song 'Die Forelle.'
A symphony orchestra's string section typically includes first violins, second violins, violas, cellos, and double basses.
Elgar's 'Enigma Variations' contains 14 variations, each depicting a friend or family member.
The Baroque period saw the invention of the modern violin family by Italian makers in Cremona.
Wagner died in Venice, Italy.
The Wagner tuba, designed by Richard Wagner for his Ring Cycle, is a hybrid between a French horn and a tuba.
Prokofiev's Symphony No. 1, the 'Classical Symphony,' was deliberately composed in the style of Haydn and Mozart.
A forte-piano (fp) marking instructs the performer to play loudly and then immediately become soft.
The Vienna New Year's Concert, broadcast to over 90 countries, features music by the Strauss family and other Viennese composers.
Rimsky-Korsakov's orchestration textbook is still used by composition students around the world.
The saxophone, though invented by Adolphe Sax in the 1840s, is rarely used in classical orchestras.
Bach's six cello suites are considered the cornerstone of the solo cello repertoire.
Beethoven's 'Emperor Concerto' (Piano Concerto No. 5) was his last piano concerto and one of the grandest.
Grieg's 'Peer Gynt Suite' includes the famous 'In the Hall of the Mountain King,' used in countless commercials and films.
The pipe organ at Notre-Dame de Paris has over 8,000 pipes and dates back to the 18th century.
Vivaldi died in poverty in Vienna, far from his native Venice, and was buried in a pauper's grave.
Brahms died in Vienna, Austria.
The gong (tam-tam) is one of the loudest percussion instruments and can produce a sustained, shimmering tone.
Shostakovich embedded his musical monogram (D-S-C-H) into many of his compositions as a personal signature.
The ondes Martenot, an early electronic instrument, was used by Messiaen in his 'Turangalîla-Symphonie.'
A concert pitch of A=415 Hz is commonly used for Baroque music, about a half step lower than modern tuning.
Verdi's 'Otello' is widely considered one of the greatest operas ever written.
The modern conductor's baton is typically made of lightweight wood, carbon fiber, or fiberglass.
Liszt composed 19 'Hungarian Rhapsodies,' each showcasing the piano's full range of expression.
Ravel died in Paris, France.
The vibraphone, developed in the 1920s, adds a shimmering, sustained quality to the percussion section.
Tchaikovsky won a commission to write his 'Piano Concerto No. 1' but its dedicatee, Nikolai Rubinstein, initially rejected it as unplayable.
Gregorian chant, named after Pope Gregory I, is one of the oldest forms of Western music, dating to the 6th century.
Chopin's études are both technical exercises and standalone concert pieces of extraordinary beauty.
The Royal Concertgebouw Orchestra of Amsterdam is one of only a handful of orchestras with its own dedicated concert hall.
Mozart was born with the baptismal name Joannes Chrysostomus Wolfgangus Theophilus Mozart.
Mahler's wife Alma was herself a composer, but Mahler asked her to give up composing when they married.
Schubert was known among his friends for intimate musical gatherings called 'Schubertiades.'
The 'prepared piano,' with objects placed between the strings, was popularized by 20th-century composer John Cage.
Rachmaninoff died in Beverly Hills, California.
The orchestral chimes (tubular bells) are long metal tubes struck with a mallet to imitate church bells.
A 'rubato' marking gives performers freedom to slightly speed up or slow down the tempo for expressive effect.
Beethoven's string quartets, especially the late ones, are considered among the most profound works in chamber music.
Britten's 'The Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra' systematically introduces each section and instrument of the orchestra.
The Classical period's emphasis on symmetry and proportion was influenced by Enlightenment ideals.
Carl Orff's 'Carmina Burana' opens with the thunderous 'O Fortuna,' one of the most dramatic choral pieces ever written.
Puccini died in Brussels, Belgium.
The natural trumpet, without valves, was the standard in orchestras until the 1820s.
Paganini's '24 Caprices for Solo Violin' remain some of the most technically demanding works for the instrument.
The 'Köchel catalogue' (K numbers) is the standard system for numbering Mozart's compositions.
Stravinsky's three early ballets — 'The Firebird,' 'Petrushka,' and 'The Rite of Spring' — were all composed for Sergei Diaghilev's Ballets Russes.
The orchestral harp weighs about 80 pounds and stands approximately six feet tall.
Debussy's opera 'Pelléas et Mélisande' uses a continuous vocal style rather than traditional arias and recitatives.
The term 'maestro' comes from the Italian word for 'master' and is a title of respect for conductors.
Mahler died in Vienna, Austria.
The 'Dies Irae' from Verdi's Requiem is one of the most powerful and dramatic movements in all choral music.
Satie's 'Gymnopédies' and 'Gnossiennes' anticipated ambient music by nearly a century.
The timpani player can change pitch by adjusting the pedal mechanism that tightens or loosens the drumhead.
Berlioz's 'Symphonie fantastique' was revolutionary for its use of a recurring theme (idée fixe) throughout all five movements.
Stradivari made his most prized instruments during his 'golden period' from about 1700 to 1720.
Handel's oratorio 'Messiah' premiered in Dublin, Ireland, not London.
A fugue subject is the main theme, and subsequent entries of the subject in different voices create an intricate musical tapestry.
The practice of applauding between movements was common in the 18th and 19th centuries but fell out of favor in the 20th century.
The Chicago Symphony Orchestra, founded in 1891, is one of the 'Big Five' American orchestras.
Bach wrote the 'Mass in B minor' over a span of about 25 years, assembling it from earlier compositions.
The 'Wolf tone' is an undesirable resonance that can occur on cellos and other string instruments.
Mozart composed 'The Marriage of Figaro' with librettist Lorenzo Da Ponte, who also wrote 'Don Giovanni' and 'Così fan tutte.'
The glass harmonica, invented by Benjamin Franklin, was used by Mozart and Beethoven in their compositions.
Dvořák died in Prague, Czech Republic.
Bernstein's televised 'Young People's Concerts' introduced classical music to millions of American children.
A scherzo, Italian for 'joke,' replaced the minuet as the third movement in many Romantic-era symphonies.
The oboe d'amore, pitched between the oboe and the English horn, was a favorite of J.S. Bach.
Copland's 'Appalachian Spring' and 'Fanfare for the Common Man' are quintessential American classical works.
Each of the four movements of Vivaldi's 'Four Seasons' is accompanied by a sonnet, possibly written by Vivaldi himself.
The BBC Proms, held at the Royal Albert Hall each summer, is the world's largest classical music festival.
Chopin's 'Ballade No. 1 in G minor' is considered one of the most technically and musically demanding piano works.
The London Symphony Orchestra, founded in 1904, was the first orchestra in Britain to be self-governing.
Mussorgsky's 'Pictures at an Exhibition' was originally a piano suite, later orchestrated by Ravel into a full orchestral showpiece.
The cor anglais solo in Dvořák's 'New World Symphony' became the melody for the hymn 'Going Home.'
Bartók collected thousands of folk melodies from Hungary, Romania, and other countries, incorporating them into his compositions.
The lowest note on a standard piano is A0, vibrating at 27.5 Hz, near the lower limit of human hearing.
